BRENT’s six-month calendar spread has slumped into a small contango for the first time since October 2025 and before that December 2023. The surge of oil exported through the re-opened Strait of Hormuz has created a temporary oversupply in the spot market. The six-month spread is currently trading in a contango of around 18 cents per barrel down from a backwardation of as much as $37 at the end of March when the fighting between the United States and Iran was at its most intense:

U.S. Gasoline Prices Are Not Being Rigged

U.S. motorists are still paying much more than normal for gasoline despite the sharp fall in crude oil prices since the United States and Iran announced their first ceasefire almost three months ago. 

“Big oil companies are not dropping their price at the pump commensurate with the sharply lower prices they are paying for oil,” President Donald Trump complained on social media on June 24. 

“Gasoline prices better start going down a lot faster than what I’m seeing,” the president warned, while announcing that he had asked the U.S. Department of Justice to investigate.  

Scapegoating producers and traders for restricting output or hoarding to push prices artificially high has been a standard response for political leaders confronted with food and fuel shortages for thousands of years.  

The president’s vague threats are mostly political theatre to show he is responding to concerns about the cost of living and not meant to be taken too seriously. 

There are good reasons why prices for gasoline have not retreated as fast as for crude oil and there is not much the government can do to force faster reductions other than suspending collection of federal gasoline taxes. 

A tax holiday would be expensive in terms of revenue losses and risk making fuel shortages worse. Only part of the tax cut would be passed on to consumers and it would probably boost refiners’ and distributors' profit margins even further ...

#Gold extended its gains after a weaker-than-expected US jobs report reinforced the dovish message delivered by Fed Chair Warsh on Wednesday. Bullion is attempting to establish a foothold above USD 4,100 after rebounding from Wednesday's intraday low of USD 3,960, with the softer payrolls data triggering a weaker dollar and a modest decline in the two-year Treasury yield to 4.11%. While yields remain well above the Fed funds target rate—indicating the market still expects another rate hike, most likely in December—the immediate pressure from rising funding-cost expectations has eased.

The recovery builds on a rebound that began after Warsh signalled inflation risks had moderated alongside falling energy prices. That view has been reinforced by the one-year US inflation swap, which has dropped sharply from a May peak of 3.5% to around 2.1%, supporting expectations that inflation pressures will continue to ease and potentially helping establish a floor under precious metals.

Despite the improvement, gold remains in a consolidation phase following its sharp correction over recent months, with some investors still using rallies to reduce exposure. Overall, the combination of lower energy prices, easing inflation expectations, a weaker dollar and softer yields suggests the market is moving closer to establishing a cyclical low.

From a technical perspective, however, more work is needed before sentiment turns decisively bullish. A daily close above USD 4,100 would be an encouraging first step, but a break above USD 4,215 is needed to improve the technical outlook. That would likely require further weakness in both the dollar and Treasury yields.

#Container freight rates continue to surge, with the Drewry Composite Index reaching a 21-month high of USD 4,530 per 40-foot container, more than doubling year-to-date, with most of the increase occurring since May.
China–US routes have led the rally during this time, with Shanghai–New York rates up 127% to around USD 7,900 per container, while Shanghai–Los Angeles rates have climbed 117%.
Early Christmas ordering and tariff uncertainty are fuelling a wave of front-loading, with retailers and importers - especially in the United States - rushing to book shipments ahead of a potential new round of US tariffs on dozens of countries expected later this month.
In addition to longer journey times, higher fuel costs linked to the Strait of Hormuz disruption are adding to freight costs, with carriers now passing higher bunker fuel expenses on to customers.
Meanwhile, the Shanghai to Rotterdam and Genoa routes have also more than doubled since May

Oil Relief for India? Goldman Sees Gulf Exports Fully Recovering by July, Keeping Crude Below $75

Global oil prices remain under pressure, with Brent crude trading below $75 a barrel, as Goldman Sachs expects Gulf oil exports to return to pre-conflict levels by July. 

Exports have already recovered to around 80% of pre-war volumes, reflecting a swift rebound after disruptions triggered by the Iran-Israel conflict. 

Easing geopolitical tensions, improved shipping access through the Gulf, and steady OPEC+ supply have strengthened market confidence.

If the recovery continues, crude prices could remain contained, reducing India's import bill, easing inflationary pressures, and improving the outlook for fuel costs.

Brent at ~$71, lowest since late February. 
Hormuz flows back above 10 million bpd. US-Iran talks progressing in Qatar. Goldman upgrades India's CY26 GDP forecast to 6.8% (from 6.5%) and cuts its CAD forecast to 1.1% of GDP.

This is what geopolitical de-escalation looks like in a portfolio. Oil was the biggest macro risk for India. It's easing. Position accordingly.

Goldman Sachs lowered its 12-month U.S. recession probability to 15%. This marks a significant reduction from the 30% forecast issued in March.

Chief economist Jan Hatzius attributed the improved outlook to a robust labor market and easing geopolitical risks. A U.S.-Iran peace agreement has specifically reduced the threat of an oil price spike.

Goldman raised its second-half 2026 GDP growth forecast to 2%. The bank expects the Federal Reserve to maintain current interest rates through the remainder of this year. However, the firm noted the Fed may consider a rate increase later in 2026.

Goldman also trimmed its year-end price forecast for gold.

Wind Financial Morning Post: July 3, 2026

Market Brief

The Ministry of Commerce: China and the EU will hold the second regular meeting of their trade and investment consultation mechanism this autumn.

China's domestic refined oil prices are set to open a new adjustment window, potentially posting the largest single cut of the year.

Yushu Technology's IPO registration on the STAR Market has been approved, paving the way for China's first listed humanoids robot company.

U.S. June non-farm payroll data came in weaker than expected, sharply lowering expectations for Federal Reserve rate hikes.

Global stock markets diverged on Thursday, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average hitting a new high while AI-related trading sank.

Anthropic's plan to develop its own AI chip triggered a sell-off in semiconductor stocks, sending SanDisk plunging 14%.

Meta's move to "sell compute capacity" shook markets, but industry insiders say the notion of compute oversupply is a misreading.

OpenAI is reportedly negotiating to offer the U.S. 
government a 5% equity stake in exchange for support.

Top Stories

The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported that non-farm payrolls increased by 57,000 in June, far below the 110,000 forecast and the lowest level in four months; April and May data were collectively revised down by 74,000. The unemployment rate unexpectedly fell to 4.2%, the lowest since June 2025, compared to an expected 4.3%. Following the data release, traders fully priced in a delay of Fed rate hikes until December.

U.S. White House National Economic Council Director Hassett said the administration is engaging with major AI companies to explore how to share benefits. Sources revealed that OpenAI has held preliminary talks with the U.S. government, proposing to offer approximately 5% equity to secure funding from the Trump administration and ease mounting regulatory pressure. OpenAI also suggested that other leading AI developers—including Anthropic, Google, and Meta—consider similar measures.

A report that Meta plans to enter the "compute-for-sale" business was widely interpreted as evidence of AI compute oversupply, triggering a global selloff in AI-related stocks on Thursday. However, multiple industry insiders argue this is a misreading: Meta's sale of compute capacity does not signal the end of AI capital spending but reflects the maturation of AI infrastructure as a business model. UBS, Morgan Stanley, and other Wall Street banks also view this not as a sign of oversupply, but as a pragmatic move by giants to balance compute constraints with financial returns. Meanwhile, JPMorgan Chase strategists warned that the current divergence between U.S. AI hardware stocks and AI investment giants resembles the pattern seen before the dot-com bubble burst.

China Macro & Policy

The Ministry of Commerce held a regular press briefing, stating that China and the EU have reached a consensus on redefining their economic relationship as stable and balanced key trading partners. The two sides will hold the second regular meeting of their trade and investment consultation mechanism this autumn. On U.S.-China agricultural trade, both sides set guiding targets to expand two-way agricultural trade and agreed in principle to include relevant products in a reciprocal tariff reduction framework.

China's Minister of Commerce Wang Wentao co-chaired the 15th China-UK Economic and Trade Joint Committee meeting with UK Minister for Trade and Investment Kier in London, exchanging in-depth views on trade, investment, and regional and multilateral cooperation. Wang said China is willing to accelerate the joint feasibility study for a China-UK services trade agreement to create favorable conditions for further enhancing bilateral services cooperation. China welcomes British enterprises to expand investment in China and hopes the UK will provide a fair, impartial, and non-discriminatory environment for Chinese firms investing in the UK.

The People's Bank of China released data on liquidity injections in June: net injection of 200 billion yuan via Medium-term Lending Facility (MLF); net withdrawal of 50 billion yuan via Pledged Supplementary Lending (PSL); net injection of 582.6 billion yuan via 7-day reverse repos; and net injection of 10 billion yuan via open market Treasury transactions.

The Ministry of Human Resources and Social Security and three other departments jointly issued a notice extending unemployment insurance stabilization refunds for enterprises that avoid or minimize layoffs until the end of 2026. The policy of one-time employment expansion subsidies will also be continued. Localities will further optimize skill enhancement subsidy policies, relaxing the required contribution period and expanding eligibility.

China Equities Markets

On Thursday, the SSE Index fell 2.03% to 4,028.9, the Shenzhen Component Index dropped 3.85%, the ChiNext Index slid 5.71%, the Star 50 Index tumbled 7.7%, and the Wind All China Index declined 2.81%. Market turnover reached 3.47 trillion yuan. On the board, previously hot sectors such as semiconductors and compute hardware suffered heavy losses, while precious metals and humanoids robotics stocks rose.

Hong Kong's main indices diverged: the Hang Seng Index rose 0.76% to 23,055.03, the Hang Seng Tech Index fell 0.4%, and the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index gained 0.72%. Semiconductor, CPO, and large model stocks plunged: Longwei Fiber Optic dropped over 22%, GigaDevice fell more than 18%, and Zhipu AI slid nearly 17%. Automobiles, biopharma, gold, and retail stocks outperformed. Net outflows from the Southbound Stock Connect totaled nearly 2 billion Hong Kong dollars.

China's first listed humanoids robot company is imminent: the China Securities Regulatory Commission approved Yushu Technology's IPO registration on the STAR Market. The company plans to raise 4.202 billion yuan. Its IPO application was accepted on March 20 and cleared the review on June 1, completing the entire process in just 104 days.

Investor enthusiasm in China's A-share market continues to grow. Shanghai Stock Exchange data show that 2.86 million new A-share accounts were opened in June, up 3.59% month-over-month and 73.99% year-over-year. A total of 201.6 million new accounts were opened in the first half of the year, a 60.04% increase from the same period last year.

China Industry News

The State Council approved the "15th Five-Year Plan for Building a Sports Powerhouse," calling for improvements to public fitness services, reforms to elite sports management, strengthened youth sports programs, revitalization of the "Big Three" ball sports, and accelerated upgrading of the sports industry.

The mandatory national standard "Safety Requirements for Combined Driving Assistance Systems in Intelligent Connected Vehicles" has been officially released and will take effect on January 1, 2027. The standard establishes a comprehensive, clear, and China-adapted safety framework for the technology.

Three mandatory national standards for the photovoltaic industry have been approved, setting rigid energy consumption and efficiency benchmarks across the entire value chain—from polysilicon and wafers to modules and inverters. The new standards aim to raise entry barriers and phase out inefficient, high-energy-consuming capacity.

At 24:00 on July 3, China's refined oil price adjustment window opens. Retail prices are expected to mark a third consecutive cut and the largest single decline of the year. According to Zaochuang Information, prices may be lowered by 855 yuan per ton, equivalent to reductions of 0.67 yuan per liter for 92-octane gasoline, 0.71 yuan for 95-octane gasoline, and 0.73 yuan for 0-diesel.

The Guangzhou Real Estate Intermediary Association reported that, due to the Dragon Boat Festival holiday and extreme heat and heavy rainfall, Guangzhou's second-hand residential transactions in June totaled 8,740 units and 8.889 million square meters, down 12.45% and 10.68% month-over-month, and down 9.82% and 7.7% year-over-year.

Hainan has introduced new housing provident fund loan policies: single contributors can now borrow up to 1 million yuan for either new or second-hand homes, and couples can borrow up to 1.2 million yuan. Support for talent loans has been strengthened, with single applicants eligible for up to 1.6 million yuan and couples up to 1.92 million yuan under qualifying conditions.

Fintech

Ant Group announced the public beta launch of its AI-powered Alipay assistant, "Abao." The initial release includes 72 intelligent service capabilities covering household management, transportation, shopping discounts, government services, financial statements, and companion interaction.

Robinhood CEO Vlad Tenev predicted that AI agents will soon match human traders in capability. In May, Robinhood rolled out tools allowing AI agents to execute stock trades and purchase goods on behalf of users.

Corporate News & Updates

NVIDIA announced a new AI infrastructure partnership model involving revenue sharing and credit support to co-build large-scale, multi-tenant AI factories with cloud service providers, enabling startups, model developers, enterprises, and research institutions to access AI compute faster while generating ongoing, usage-linked revenue for NVIDIA.

Sources say Anthropic has begun planning its own AI chip project and is in discussions with Samsung Electronics on potential manufacturing partnerships. The project remains in early stages, with no final decisions on purpose, performance targets, or integration into servers.

Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg said AI agent development has not accelerated as expected over the past four months. The company's 2026 restructuring "could have been cleaner," and its bets on the reorganization "have yet to pay off."

Sources indicate Apple has raised its production target for its first foldable iPhone by 30% to approximately 10 million units this year. The company has locked in components for about 80 million new iPhones and requested suppliers to reserve generic components for next year's iPhone 18 series in advance.

The European Court of Justice dismissed Google's appeal, upholding a ruling that Google abused its dominant position in the mobile market via Android, and maintained a €4.1 billion antitrust fine.

Microsoft announced the formation of a new company to help businesses understand and deploy generative AI, with an initial funding commitment of $2.5 billion and approximately 6,000 employees embedded directly into client organizations.

Tesla CEO Elon Musk posted a photo of himself with the Optimus humanoid robot production team, sparking speculation of accelerated production. He later tempered expectations, saying, "Initial Optimus production will be extremely slow because everything is new. This is not like building a car."

Tesla delivered 480,100 vehicles globally in the second quarter, a 25% year-over-year increase and well above the 396,500 forecast. Energy storage deployments reached 13.5 GWh, up about 41%. Tesla launched a long-wheelbase Model Y in the U.S. and Puerto Rico.

According to an internal memo, Tesla has set a weekly AI usage spending cap of $200 per employee, effective July 6.

SoftBank Group and SoftBank Corp. announced they will establish a joint venture to operate a new cloud business, planning to begin offering AI compute services to U.S. enterprises in fiscal year 2027. Sources say if the U.S. cloud business scales successfully, SoftBank Corp.'s annual operating profit could rise to 3 to 4 trillion yen.

Alibaba is integrating its agent product lines, combining QoderWork, Wukong, and MuleRun into a unified AI product targeting enterprise productivity. DingTalk CEO Chen Yusen will lead the new product.

Global Macro & Policy

The U.S. National Weather Service warned that record-breaking heat will persist across much of the central and eastern U.S., with peak heat index temperatures reaching 115°F (46°C). Approximately 163 million people are affected. The heat is straining power grids; the Department of Energy has authorized the use of diesel and natural gas generators to reduce reliance on the public grid.

Oman opposes levying transit fees on vessels passing through the Strait of Hormuz but remains open to discussing maritime service-related charges. Meanwhile, several major European nations acknowledge that charging fees for passage through the strait is now unavoidable. Reports indicate Oman has proposed a service fee scheme for ships transiting the strait.

The U.S. government is accelerating negotiations with OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and other leading AI firms to launch a voluntary safety framework for advanced AI models, potentially as early as next week. The framework will establish safety benchmarks and timelines for models with frontier capabilities, reshaping compliance costs and product development cycles for AI companies.

Senator Elizabeth Warren wrote to Federal Reserve Governor Waller urging him to halt proposed reforms to the 12 regional Federal Reserve Bank structures. Warren argued the reforms appear designed to appease President Trump and risk undermining the Fed's independence.

San Francisco Fed President Daly said while inflation is expected to gradually decline, economic prospects remain highly uncertain. If inflation persists above target, the Fed may need to respond more aggressively to ensure price stability.

The South Korean government disclosed it will invest a total of 392 trillion won ($252.5 billion) in the Chungcheong region, primarily to build HBM wafer fabs and chip packaging facilities. Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix will invest 140 trillion won and 100 trillion won respectively, while other firms will invest about 150 trillion won in AI data centers and related areas.

South Korea's June CPI rose 3.2% year-over-year, the largest increase since December 2023 and the second consecutive month above the central bank's 2% target, raising market expectations for a rate hike.

Global Equity Markets

U.S. major indices closed mixed: the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 1.14% to 52,900.07, a new record high; the S&P 500 edged flat at 7,483.24; the Nasdaq Composite fell 0.8% to 25,832.67. Apple rose nearly 5%, McDonald's gained over 4%, leading the Dow. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index dropped 5.44%, SanDisk plunged over 14%, and Micron fell over 5%. The Wind US Magnificent Seven Index declined 0.11%. Tesla slid over 7%, Meta fell nearly 5%. SpaceX rose nearly 3%. The Nasdaq China Golden Dragon Index dropped 1.77%, 21Vianet fell over 10%, and Bawang Cha Ji declined over 8%.

European major indices closed higher: Germany's DAX rose 2.16% to 25,580.88; France's CAC 40 climbed 1.65% to 8,474.86; the UK's FTSE 100 gained 1.67% to 10,652.87. The weaker-than-expected U.S. non-farm payroll data reduced Fed rate hike bets, while Germany's ruling coalition reached a comprehensive reform package, boosting market confidence.

South Korea's KOSPI plunged 7.89% to 7,648.09, triggering a circuit breaker intraday. Japan's Nikkei 225 fell 2.47% to 68,733.15. AI leaders including SK Hynix, Samsung Electronics, Kioxia, and Tokyo Electron all slid sharply. India's SENSEX 30 rose 0.75% to 77,502.12.

Bonds

China's interbank bond market traded flat overall, with yields on key government bonds fluctuating by less than 1 basis point; longer-dated Treasuries (7+ years) outperformed. Treasury futures edged higher. Liquidity in the interbank market improved, with the PBOC conducting 288.5 billion yuan in reverse repo operations and net withdrawing 82 billion yuan.

Japan's Finance Ministry data showed overseas investors sold 3.12 trillion yen ($19.2 billion) in Japanese bonds in June, the largest monthly outflow since January 2023. On July 2, the subscription ratio for a 10-year JGB auction fell to 3.13, pressuring long-dated bonds, with the 20-year yield spiking as much as 11 basis points.

U.S. Treasury yields moved mixed: the 2-year yield fell 5.18 basis points to 4.123%; the 3-year yield dropped 3.42 basis points to 4.150%; the 5-year yield declined 2.10 basis points to 4.214%; the 10-year yield eased 0.99 basis point to 4.467%; the 30-year yield rose 0.20 basis point to 4.972%.

Commodities

International precious metals futures rose broadly: COMEX gold futures gained 1.30% to $4,135.50 per ounce; COMEX silver futures rose 1.54% to $61.44 per ounce. Lower Fed rate hike expectations, weak non-farm data, continued central bank gold buying, and risk-off sentiment from A-share declines supported prices.

U.S. crude oil futures fell 0.17% to $68.46 per barrel; Brent crude edged down 0.01% to $71.56 per barrel. Tensions in the Middle East eased, with crude shipments through the Strait of Hormuz rebounding sharply, increasing supply expectations. Multiple institutions have lowered oil price forecasts.

Most base metals on the LME closed lower: LME aluminum rose 0.23% to $3,083.00 per tonne; LME lead rose 0.16% to $1,868.50 per tonne; LME copper fell 0.10% to $13,285.50 per tonne; LME nickel dropped 0.37% to $16,295.00 per tonne; LME zinc fell 0.76% to $3,472.50 per tonne; LME tin declined 1.50% to $50,855.00 per tonne.

FX & Currency Markets

On Thursday, the onshore renminbi closed at 6.7866 against the U.S. dollar, up 69 basis points from the previous close; the night session ended at 6.7830. The PBOC's mid-rate was set at 6.8088, down 21 basis points from the prior day.

At New York's close, the U.S. Dollar Index fell 0.54% to 100.87. Most non-U.S. currencies rose: the euro climbed 0.49% to 1.1434; the pound gained 0.54% to 1.3347; the Australian dollar rose 0.42% to 0.6923; the dollar-yen fell 0.91% to 161.1190; the dollar-Canadian dollar declined 0.24% to 1.4182; the dollar-swiss franc dropped 0.75% to 0.8035; the offshore renminbi rose 65 basis points to 6.7882.

Sources say Japanese officials are abandoning the traditional practice of signaling intervention risks in advance, opting instead for targeted, surprise interventions to deter speculators and raise the cost of shorting the yen. Officials are also avoiding indicating specific exchange rate thresholds that might trigger action.

冰火两重天！道指创新高，芯片股拖累纳指，闪迪重挫14.13%，中概股普跌，国际油价走高

*美股涨跌互现，道指创新高

*芯片股哀鸿遍野，闪迪跌14.13%，美光跌超5%

*美国6月新增5.7万个非农就业岗位

周四（2日）美股三大股指涨跌互现。投资者对弱于预期的6月非农就业报告作出反应，道指攀升至历史新高，而纳指则因芯片股再度承压而表现低迷。

截至收盘，道指涨594.83点，涨幅1.14%，报52900.07点；纳指跌207.36点，跌幅0.80%，报25832.67点；标普500指数涨0.01点，涨幅接近于零，报7483.24点。

【热门股表现】

美股龙头科技股涨跌互现。苹果公司收涨4.84%，奈飞涨4.66%，SpaceX涨2.83%，微软涨1.62%，亚马逊涨0.40%。下跌阵营中，英伟达收跌1.39%，谷歌跌0.36%。

特斯拉重挫7.49%。这家电动汽车制造商公布的第二季度交付量和产量远高于华尔街预期，在汽车销量连年下滑后，这家车企仍在努力重振旗鼓。

text: @dazzaecg @DMPR_ZA I’m quite amazed at the number of folks on this website that cannot comprehend the relationship between Brent crude, and the cost of fuel products.

MARKET UPDATE — FRIDAY, 3 JULY 2026

Wall Street received the weaker jobs report it had been waiting for—but investors did not react as though every part of the market was suddenly safe.

The Dow reached another record as fears of an immediate Federal Reserve interest-rate increase eased.

Yet semiconductor shares suffered another heavy sell-off, the Nasdaq declined, the yen remained close to a 40-year low, and investors continued questioning whether extraordinary AI expectations have moved too far ahead of reality.

The message from markets was unusually clear:

The economy is slowing—but inflation, valuation and financial-system risks have not disappeared.

🟢 US HIRING SLOWS SHARPLY

The United States added only 57,000 jobs in June, substantially below expectations of approximately 110,000.

The previous two months were also weaker than first reported:

🔸 April and May payrolls were revised down by a combined 74,000.

🔸 Leisure and hospitality lost 61,000 jobs.

🔸 Hiring remained concentrated in areas including professional services, healthcare and social assistance.

The unemployment rate nevertheless fell from 4.3% to 4.2%.

That sounds positive—but the explanation is less encouraging.

A large number of people stopped working or looking for work, causing the labour-force participation rate to fall to 61.5%, its lowest level in more than five years.

Simple explanation:

The unemployment rate counts only people who are actively looking for work.

When people stop searching, they disappear from the unemployment calculation. The rate can therefore fall even when the jobs market is becoming weaker.

The US economy has still created an average of approximately 92,000 jobs per month during the first half of 2026, a major improvement from the average monthly losses experienced during the second half of 2025.

This is not yet a collapsing labour market.

It looks more like a slow-hire, slow-fire economy—stable enough to avoid an immediate recession, but no longer strong enough to justify aggressive interest-rate increases without considerable risk.

🟢 THE FED’S DILEMMA BECOMES MORE COMPLICATED

The weaker jobs report reduced expectations that the Federal Reserve will raise rates imminently.

The two-year Treasury yield eased to approximately 4.13%, below its recent peak near 4.23%, as investors reconsidered how much further interest rates may need to rise.

Simple explanation:

Short-term bond yields reflect where investors expect Federal Reserve rates to move.

When those yields fall, markets are usually becoming less convinced that rates must increase.

However, one weak employment report does not guarantee lower interest rates.

US inflation recently exceeded 4%, and average wages still increased approximately 3.5% over the past year.

President Donald Trump again argued for lower rates and described the Federal Reserve board facing Chair Kevin Warsh as “hostile”.

But the Fed’s decision should depend on inflation and employment—not political pressure.

Warsh has indicated that he pays close attention to “trimmed mean” inflation measures, which remove the largest price increases and decreases to reveal the underlying trend.

These measures can be useful—but they also carry a danger.

When families are genuinely paying much more for food, fuel and essential goods, removing those increases from an inflation measure can make the economy appear more comfortable than it feels in real life.

It is like measuring the temperature of a house while excluding the rooms that are overheating.

The core problem remains unresolved:

🔸 Weak employment argues against higher rates.

🔸 Persistent inflation argues against lower rates.

The most likely near-term outcome is therefore patience rather than an immediate policy reversal.

🟢 THE DOW HITS A RECORD—BUT TECHNOLOGY FALLS

The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 1.14% to a record close of 52,900.

The S&P 500 finished almost exactly unchanged at 7,483.

The Nasdaq Composite fell 0.80% to 25,833.

This was not a broad market celebration.

Investors rotated towards older-economy, defensive and financially sensitive companies while taking profits from semiconductor and AI-infrastructure shares.

The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index fell approximately 5.4%, extending its decline for a second day.

🔸 Nvidia fell 1.4%.

🔸 SanDisk dropped 14.1%.

🔸 The semiconductor index has still gained roughly 78% during 2026, leaving substantial profits available to be taken.

Apple provided an important counterweight, rising approximately 4.8% following reports that it plans to introduce five new iPhone models.

Investors were moving money from some of the year’s biggest winners into other parts of the market.

Healthcare, financials and industrial companies have recently performed better than many AI-infrastructure shares.

Some of Thursday’s strongest non-technology performers included:

🏥 Healthcare

🔸 Moderna rose approximately 10.0%.

🔸 Universal Health Services gained 5.2%.

🔸 HCA Healthcare advanced 4.4%.

🏦 Financials

🔸 Blue Owl Capital rose approximately 4.6%.

🔸 Mastercard gained 3.2%.

🔸 Visa advanced 3.1%.

🏭 Industrials

🔸 AeroVironment surged approximately 11.0% after securing a $500 million US government contract.

Simple explanation:

Money did not leave the stock market entirely.

It moved from some of the year’s most heavily owned AI and semiconductor shares into healthcare, financial and industrial companies.

The broadening of the rally could make it healthier—but it could also indicate that investors are becoming less comfortable with the prices and expectations attached to semiconductor companies.

🟢 ARE AI EARNINGS EXPECTATIONS BECOMING TOO HIGH?

Wall Street analysts now expect S&P 500 earnings to increase approximately 25% over the coming year.

Profit forecasts have risen almost 20% in only six months—the fastest upgrade cycle since the post-pandemic recovery.

Much of that optimism rests on AI, semiconductor demand and hyperscaler investment.

The positive argument is straightforward:

🔸 AI infrastructure spending remains enormous.

🔸 Corporate profits remain resilient.

🔸 Computing demand continues to expand.

🔸 Stronger expected earnings make today’s market valuations appear less extreme.

But the danger is that share prices increasingly depend on analysts’ optimistic forecasts becoming reality.

AI-related companies are being valued as though exceptionally high growth and profit margins can continue for years.

That leaves very little room for disappointment.

A slowdown in AI demand, rising construction and electricity costs, weaker pricing power, or difficulty turning capital expenditure into revenue could force analysts to reduce their forecasts.

Simple explanation:

A company’s share price can look reasonable when investors expect its profits to rise rapidly.

But if those future profits fail to arrive, the apparently reasonable valuation can suddenly look expensive.

The risk may therefore be less of a traditional “price bubble” and more of an expectations bubble.

🟢 AI’S NEXT BOTTLENECK MAY NOT BE CHIPS

At least 75 US data-centre projects worth approximately $130 billion were reportedly blocked or delayed during the first quarter.

Local communities are increasingly concerned about:

🔸 electricity consumption;

🔸 higher household power bills;

🔸 water usage;

🔸 constant noise;

🔸 environmental damage;

🔸 and the limited number of permanent jobs created after construction ends.

Blackstone-owned QTS has now abandoned its long-planned Digital Gateway data-centre project in Virginia following years of opposition and legal challenges.

For the past two years, investors have asked whether companies could manufacture enough AI chips.

The next question may be:

Can they obtain enough electricity, land, water and political permission to operate those chips?

Simple explanation:

Having thousands of advanced chips is like owning thousands of aircraft.

They are useless without airports, fuel, electricity and permission to fly.

Local resistance does not end the AI boom, but it could make infrastructure more expensive, delay revenue and favour the largest companies capable of navigating regulation and securing their own power supplies.

🟢 META ADMITS AI AGENTS ARE TAKING LONGER

Mark Zuckerberg reportedly told Meta employees that AI-agent development has not accelerated as quickly as the company expected.

AI agents are systems designed not merely to answer questions, but to perform complex tasks independently.

Meta continues investing heavily and expects more meaningful progress within the next three to six months.

The admission matters because it separates two different parts of the AI story:

🔸 Building more computing infrastructure is happening rapidly.

🔸 Turning that infrastructure into reliable, profitable autonomous products is proving harder.

This does not mean AI has failed.

It means investors may need to distinguish between technological progress and immediate commercial returns.

The companies building the infrastructure are spending money today.

The profits promised by autonomous AI agents may arrive later—and with greater uncertainty.

🟢 TESLA DELIVERS RECORD VEHICLE NUMBERS—BUT SHARES FALL

Tesla delivered 480,126 vehicles during the second quarter, approximately 25% more than a year earlier and ahead of market expectations.

The company produced 451,758 vehicles and deployed a record 13.5 GWh of energy-storage products.

Despite those strong figures, Tesla shares fell approximately 7.5%.

Why?

The stock had risen sharply before the report as traders anticipated strong deliveries.

Once the numbers arrived, some investors took profits.

This is a classic example of “buy the rumour, sell the news”.

Simple explanation:

A company can publish good results and still see its share price fall when investors had already expected—or priced in—even better news.

Tesla’s longer-term valuation increasingly depends on more than vehicle sales.

Investors are also paying for potential future earnings from:

🔸 Full Self-Driving;

🔸 robotaxis;

🔸 the Cybercab;

🔸 Optimus humanoid robots;

🔸 AI computing;

🔸 and energy storage.

Tesla plans to spend more than $25 billion this year developing these businesses.

The opportunity is enormous—but many of these investments could take years to generate meaningful profits.

🟢 JAPAN’S WAGES RAISE THE RISK OF A YEN CARRY-TRADE UNWIND

The Japanese yen recently weakened beyond ¥162 per US dollar, its lowest level since 1986.

That has encouraged investors to borrow cheaply in yen and invest the money in higher-returning US shares, bonds and other assets.

This strategy is known as the yen carry trade.

It tends to work while:

🔸 Japanese borrowing costs remain low;

🔸 the yen remains weak;

🔸 and global risk assets continue rising.

But the trade can reverse violently if the yen suddenly strengthens.

Japan’s annual wage negotiations have now produced average pay rises of more than 5% for a third consecutive year—an important change after decades of weak wage growth and low inflation.

Higher wages could support Japanese consumer spending and corporate earnings.

However, they also give the Bank of Japan more justification to raise interest rates or maintain tighter monetary policy.

That matters globally because higher Japanese rates would make borrowing in yen less attractive.

If Japan intervenes in the currency market—or if the Bank of Japan tightens policy—investors may be forced to:

🔸 buy yen to repay their loans;

🔸 sell US technology shares and other investments;

🔸 and reduce leveraged positions rapidly.

That is similar to what contributed to the sharp global market decline during 2024.

Simple explanation:

Imagine borrowing money in a currency that keeps becoming cheaper.

The trade feels easy while the currency stays weak.

But if that currency suddenly rises, the debt becomes more expensive and investors may have to sell other assets quickly to repay it.

Japan has already spent approximately ¥11.7 trillion supporting the yen during April and May.

Thin trading conditions around the US Independence Day holiday could make any intervention unusually powerful.

The present carry trade appears less crowded than it was in 2024, and investors are more aware of the danger.

That could limit the fallout.

But the key point is this:

Japan’s wage growth, Bank of Japan policy, the yen and US technology valuations are all parts of the same global liquidity chain.

🟢 PRIVATE CREDIT FACES RISING WITHDRAWAL PRESSURE

Investors requested approximately $15.6 billion from major private-credit funds during the second quarter.

Fund managers returned only around $5.9 billion, while new investment entering the sector slowed sharply.

This does not necessarily mean that these funds have lost the money.

The underlying problem is liquidity.

Private-credit funds lend to companies through loans that do not trade every day.

When many investors request their cash simultaneously, managers cannot sell those loans as easily as a publicly traded share or government bond.

Withdrawal limits prevent a fire sale—but they also remind investors that “private” assets may be difficult to exit during periods of stress.

Why this matters:

Private credit has become an important source of financing for medium-sized and lower-rated businesses.

If withdrawals continue while new inflows weaken, funds may have less money available to:

🔸 refinance corporate debt;

🔸 support business expansion;

🔸 rescue struggling borrowers;

🔸 or make new loans.

That could tighten financial conditions even without the Federal Reserve increasing interest rates.

Private credit is not yet a systemic crisis.

But it is a growing pressure point that deserves close attention.

🟢 FOOD INFLATION REMAINS PAINFUL

The estimated cost of a typical US Independence Day barbecue is reportedly approximately 9.4% higher than last year.

Beef prices have risen by more than 17%, reflecting historically low cattle supplies, transport costs, expensive agricultural inputs and earlier energy disruptions.

Oil has fallen sharply from its wartime peak, with Brent trading near $72 a barrel.

That should eventually reduce transport and production costs.

But falling oil prices do not immediately reverse food inflation.

Farmers, manufacturers, transport companies and supermarkets often pass earlier cost increases through the supply chain for months.

For ordinary households, the economic picture therefore remains very different from the stock market’s record highs.

Affluent consumers benefiting from rising investment portfolios continue spending on luxury goods and travel.

Lower- and middle-income households remain more dependent on discount retailers and sensitive to food, rent and borrowing costs.

This two-speed economy helps explain why markets can look extraordinarily strong while many families still feel financially worse off.

🟢 ASIAN MARKETS REBOUND

Asian shares recovered on Friday as investors reduced expectations of an immediate US interest-rate increase.

At the time of reporting:

🔸 the MSCI Asia-Pacific index excluding Japan rose approximately 2.2%;

🔸 South Korea’s Kospi surged more than 6% as investors returned to battered semiconductor shares;

🔸 Japan’s Nikkei gained approximately 1.2%;

🔸 S&P 500 futures rose around 0.4%;

🔸 Brent crude traded near $72.26;

🔸 gold rose to approximately $4,174;

🔸 Bitcoin traded near $61,819;

🔸 and Ether remained around $1,741.

Regional economic data also remained broadly constructive.

Japan’s services sector returned to expansion, while Chinese services continued growing and overseas demand increased at its fastest pace in approximately 20 months.

These figures suggest that Asian economic activity has not collapsed despite recent market volatility.

🟢 WHAT IT MEANS FOR INVESTORS

Friday’s market environment contains several contradictory signals:

🔸 Hiring is slowing, reducing the immediate need for higher US rates.

🔸 Inflation remains too high for the Fed to declare victory.

🔸 The Dow is reaching records while semiconductor shares correct sharply.

🔸 AI investment remains enormous, but infrastructure and commercialisation obstacles are becoming clearer.

🔸 Tesla’s deliveries are strong, but future expectations remain even stronger.

🔸 Japan’s weak currency supports carry trades, while intervention and rising wages threaten to unwind them.

🔸 Private credit continues lending, but investor withdrawals are increasing.

This is not a market in which investors should abandon risk.

Nor is it one in which they should assume every dip is automatically safe.

The rational response is disciplined risk management:

🔸 avoid excessive leverage;

🔸 distinguish strong businesses from strong stories;

🔸 examine whether earnings forecasts are realistic;

🔸 rebalance after extraordinary gains;

🔸 and keep sufficient liquidity for volatility.

The second half of 2026 may not be decided simply by whether AI continues growing.

It may be decided by whether AI profits can grow quickly enough to justify the expectations already embedded in asset prices—while inflation, interest rates and global liquidity remain restrictive.

💭 THOUGHT FOR THE DAY

“Great investors do not predict every wave. They build a vessel strong enough to survive the ones they never saw coming.”

Sources: 
US Bureau of Labor Statistics; Reuters; MarketWatch; The Wall Street Journal; Financial Times; Tesla Investor Relations; Data Center Watch; CME FedWatch; FactSet; Bloomberg; Barron's.

Crude Inventory Hits 1-yr High on Stronger Imports

What happened?
India's crude oil inventory has surged to its highest level in a year. The significant spike in oil stockpiles is primarily driven by a robust wave of stronger crude imports hitting the country, coupled with a notable shift in global supply dynamics—including deeply discounted or higher volumes of international supplies flowing into the market.

What it means?
The surge in stockpiles ensures a very healthy buffer for domestic refiners, safeguarding India's energy security against short-term global supply disruptions. This influx of crude coincides with rising domestic fuel demands (with petrol sales up 7% and diesel up 5.5%), ensuring that local refineries remain well-stocked to maintain high utilization rates and keep the domestic market sufficiently supplied.

The Catch
While higher inventories provide a domestic cushion, they are accumulating at a time when global crude prices have faced a downward slide. For state-run oil marketing companies (OMCs), processing this higher-volume inventory amid lower international crude prices has squeezed margins, leading to estimated losses of around ₹75,000 crore in Q1 due to lower market prices.

Bottomline
While the surge in crude imports strengthens India's near-term energy security and fuels rising domestic transport demands, the resulting inventory build-up amid falling global oil prices is dealing a heavy financial blow to state oil companies' quarterly earnings.

Dangote Refinery Clarifies: Pricing Not Tied to Daily International Crude Oil Quotations

In its official statement, the refinery clarified that its pricing does not move in tandem with daily international crude oil quotations. Added that Crude oil is procured weeks and in some cases months before it is processed, under commercial contracts linked primarily to monthly average pricing mechanisms rather than prevailing spot market prices. 

The statement reads: “Dangote Petroleum Refinery & Petrochemicals remains committed to ensuring that Nigerians benefit from favourable market developments through fair, responsible and sustainable pricing of petroleum products.
Since May 30, 2026, Dangote Petroleum Refinery has reduced the ex-depot price of Premium Motor Spirit (PMS) by N200 per litre, Automotive Gas Oil (AGO) by N300 per litre and Jet A1 aviation fuel by N520 per litre, despite continuing to process crude oil acquired during a period when international crude prices were significantly higher than current levels.

“These reductions demonstrate our commitment to passing on cost efficiencies to consumers while maintaining the operational and financial sustainability of domestic refining.

“It is important to clarify that refinery pricing does not move in tandem with daily international crude oil quotations. Crude oil is procured weeks, and in some cases months, before it is processed, under commercial contracts linked primarily to monthly average pricing mechanisms rather than prevailing spot market prices.

“Consequently, the petroleum products currently being supplied from our refinery are being produced from crude inventories acquired at substantially higher costs than today's market prices. The average landed cost of crude processed by the refinery was approximately US$124.80 per barrel in May and US$95.25 per barrel in June, compared with the current international benchmark of about US$71.01 per barrel.

“Furthermore, refinery feedstock is not purchased at the headline ICE Brent price commonly reported in the media. Our crude is acquired on a Dated Brent plus market premium, freight and logistics cost basis, resulting in actual landed costs that differ materially from benchmark quotations.

“Notwithstanding these elevated feedstock costs, Dangote Petroleum Refinery did not immediately transfer the full impact of rising crude prices to the Nigerian market. Instead, the refinery absorbed a substantial portion of the increase in order to support market stability, reduce inflationary pressures and shield consumers from the extreme volatility witnessed in global energy markets. For this reason, prices of petroleum products in Nigeria are still lower than prices in neighbouring countries even after adjusting for taxes.

“As lower cost crude cargoes progressively enter our production cycle, we have commenced a phased reduction in product prices.

“Today's N50 per litre reduction is the fourth price cut in one month, bringing cumulative reductions to above N200 per litre on PMS. This approach ensures that pricing decisions are anchored on actual production economics and inventory costs rather than short term fluctuations in international oil markets.

“Nigeria today benefits from the stabilising role of domestic refining capacity. The Dangote Petroleum Refinery currently supplies volumes sufficient to meet national demand, helping to strengthen energy security, eliminate dependence on imports, conserve foreign exchange and provide greater price stability for consumers and businesses.

“As procurement costs continue to decline and lower priced inventories replace higher cost crude stocks, Nigerians can expect further price moderation, provided international market conditions remain favourable.”

The Clearwater Bay (OBO), IMO No. 7305241, was originally built as the Alva Bay in 1973 by the Gotaverken Arendal shipyard in Gothenburg, Sweden.

Designed as an ore and oil carrier, it measured 332.57 meters in length and 45.67 meters in width, with a deadweight tonnage of 225,898 metric tons.

Powered by a single steam turbine, the ship flew the British flag and served during an era when mixed-use vessels were employed to transport both dry bulk cargo and crude oil on long-distance international routes.

In 1983, the ship was renamed Clearwater Bay and came under the flag of Pu Tai Shipping Co., Ltd.

Toward the end of its service life, the ship played a role in a well-known engineering solution used during the construction of the land reclamation project in the Seosan Sea (often referred to as the Seosan Farm) in 1984 in South Korea.

The coastal development project aimed to create thousands of hectares of new farmland by enclosing the mudflats with a long dike. One of the main challenges was closing the final breach in the dike, where powerful tidal currents made conventional construction techniques extremely difficult.
Engineers solved the problem by deliberately sinking a decommissioned oil tanker through the remaining opening.

Why sink an oil tanker?
The submerged oil tanker served as a temporary barrier that:

The speed of the tidal currents was reduced.
Construction equipment was protected from the strongest currents.

Calmer waters were created to allow for the precise placement of rocks and earth fill.

It served as a stable core around which the final section of the dike could be completed.

Once the current had weakened sufficiently, construction crews quickly placed large quantities of rock and fill material until the dam was fully closed.

Why was this important?
The use of a decommissioned oil tanker served to:
Be faster than constructing temporary cofferdams in waters with very strong tidal currents.
Less expensive than many other methods.

An innovative example of adapting existing infrastructure for civil engineering purposes.
The successful closure of the dam enabled the completion of one of South Korea’s largest land reclamation projects, creating approximately 6,400 hectares (64 km²) of farmland reclaimed from the sea. This project has become a benchmark in coastal engineering and land reclamation.

Its operational life ended when it was dismantled on June 4, 1984.

अब बदल चुकी है स्थिति

अब स्थिति बदल चुकी है।अमेरिका और ईरान में जंग थम गई है,लेकिन विश्व की तेल जरूरत के 20 फीसदी की आपूर्ति के लिए अहम समुद्री रूट होर्मुज स्ट्रेट अभी पूरी तरह खुला नहीं है,फिर भी तेल की कीमतें बिखरी हुई नजर आ रही हैं।ऐसे में अमेरिकी राष्ट्रपति डोनाल्ड ट्रंप की कच्चे तेल की कीमत वाली भविष्यवाणी सच होती दिखी है,जबकि कच्चे तेल में कई ग्लोबल ब्रोकरेज के अनुमानों के मुताबिक गिरावट देखने को मिल रही है।

कच्चे तेल की कीमतों में गिरावट,राहत में विश्व

अंतरराष्ट्रीय स्तर पर कच्चे तेल की कीमतों में गिरावट का सिलसिला लगातार जारी है।गुरुवार को अपडेटेड कच्चे तेल की कीमत पर नजर डालें तो इंटरनेशनल मार्केट में ब्रेंट क्रूड ऑयल का दाम 70.60 डॉलर प्रति बैरल पर ट्रेड कर रहा था। इसके अलावा ब्रेंट क्रूड ऑयल दाम फिसलकर 67 डॉलर प्रति बैरल के आसपास आ गया था।मर्बन क्रूड ऑयल प्राइस 65 डॉलर पर ट्रेड करता नजर आया।

ट्रंप के सिग्नल,ब्रोकरेज की भविष्यवाणी

अमेरिका और ईरान में जंग जब चरम पर थी और कच्चा तेल 110 डॉलर प्रति बैरल के पार चल रहा था,तो मई महीने में डोनाल्ड ट्रंप ने जंग खत्म होने से जुड़े सिग्नल दिए थे।ट्रंप ने सोशल मीडिया प्लेटफॉर्म ट्रुथ सोशल पर एक पोस्ट में कहा था कि कच्चे तेल की कीमतें तेजी से गिरेंगी और ईरान युद्ध जल्द समाप्त होगा।ट्रंप ने भविष्यवाणी करते हुए कहा था कि ईरान हमसे समझौते को बेताब है,वहां तेल का बड़ा भंडार है,ऐसा होने पर Crude Oil की ग्लोबल कीमतों में भारी गिरावट आएगी।

ट्रंप के संकेतों के बाद ब्रोकरेज फर्मों ने तेल की कीमतें गिरने का जाहिर किए थे अनुमान

ट्रंप के संकेतों के बाद ब्रोकरेज फर्मों ने भी तेल की कीमतें गिरने का अनुमान जाहिर किए थे।एमके ग्लोबल ने कहा था कि 2027 की पहली छमाही के बाद कीमतों में गिरावट स्थायी आनी चाहिए और वित्त वर्ष के अंत तक तेल की कीमतें गिरकर 70 डॉलर प्रति बैरल तक पहुंच सकती हैं।ब्रोकरेज का ये अनुमान टाइमलाइन से बहुत पहले ही सच हो गया है।

ट्रंप के ऐलान के बाद कच्चा तेल धड़ाम

मई में ट्रंप ने तेल की कीमतें कम होने की भविष्यवाणी की थी और जून में अमेरिका और ईरान के बीच 14 सूत्रीय शांति प्रस्ताव पर बात बनी।ट्रंप ने समझौते पर सहमति से जुड़ा बयान जारी किया कि अमेरिकी नाकाबंदी हटाई जा रही है और होर्मुज फिर से खुलने वाला है।ट्रंप के ऐलान के बाद अचानक कच्चे तेल की कीमत कम होती चला गईं और अगले ही दिन 80 डॉलर के आसपास आ गयी थी।

होर्मुज पूरी तरह ओपन नहीं,फिर क्यों तेल सस्ता

अमेरिका और ईरान के ऐलान के बाद अचानक बीते दिनों फिर से दोनों देशों में जंग की खबर आई।ईरान ने कुवैत और बहरीन में अमेरिकी सैन्य अड्डों पर मिसाइल और ड्रोन अटैक किए।इससे होर्मुज को लेकर टेंशन फिर से बढ़ी।हालांकि ताजा अपडेट देखें तो कतर की राजधानी दोहा में अमेरिका और ईरान के बीच अहम वार्ता में दोनों पक्ष अगली बैठक पर सहमत हुए हैं।कतर विदेश मंत्रालय ने कहा है कि कई मुद्दों पर सकारात्मक प्रगति हुई है,इनमें होर्मुज भी शामिल है।

बता दें कि अमेरिका-ईरान के बीच युद्ध थमा है,लेकिन होर्मुज अभी भी पूरी तरह से खुला नहीं है।कुछ जहाजों की आवाजाही रिकॉर्ड की गई है,इसके बावजूद भी कच्चे तेल की कीमतों में तेज गिरावट की वजह देखें, तो तेल बाजार मौजूदा स्थिति के बजाय आने वाले समय की संभावनाओं के आधार पर कीमतें तय करता है।दोनों देशों की बातचीत में प्रगति से सप्लाई में रुकावट का खतरा भी कम हुआ है,जिसके बाद बाजार ने रिस्क प्रीमियम कम किया है,इसके अलावा OPEC+ प्रोडक्शन में बढ़ोतरी कर रहा है, ज्यादा उत्पादन से अधिक सप्लाई होती है और कीमतों पर दबाव पड़ता है।तमाम देशों ने जहां अपने स्ट्रेटेजिक ऑयल रिजर्व में बढ़ोतरी की है, तो वहीं UAE, सऊदी अरब जैसे तेल उत्पादक देशों के उत्पादन में बढ़ोतरी की उम्मीद से बाजार में सप्लाई सुचारू रहने की उम्मीद जागी है।
